coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots crosslinked Salix psammophila microcrystalline cellulose interpenetrating hydrogel system for sensitive detection and efficient adsorption of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Boyun
Zhong, Yuan
Liu, Zhechen
Zhang, Wanqi
Hu, Zichu
Chen, Long
Wang, Ximing
Zhang, Xiaotao
coredata.dc:description: To combat the contaminating effects of heavy-metal ions on water resources, we prepared blue photonitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) from Salix psammophila (SP) as a raw material, doped them into a microcrystalline cellulose hydrogel, and successfully created a composite hydrogel (NCDs-SP-MCC-gels) for concurrent fluorescence detection and Fe3+ adsorption. The NCDs-SP-MCC-gels was examined for their shape, chemical composition, fluorescence characteristics, and adsorption behaviors. Results demonstrated that the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels displayed a broad linear range of Fe3+ fluorescence response (200–1000 mg/L; detection limit = 3.90583 mg/L) and a high adsorption capacity for Fe3+ (610 mg/g). The adsorption data fitted the Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models well. Further research was conducted on Fe3+ detection and adsorption onto the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. This study proposes a green and sustainable way to create a material for the detection and absorption of heavy-metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose
Carbon quantum dots
Fe3+
Detection
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing capabilities of carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Innovative Entrepreneurship Training Program of Inner Mongolia
Central Government Guides Local Science and Technology Development Fund Projects

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Interdisciplinary Research Fund of Inner Mongolia Agricultural University ( BR22-15-02 ), the Central Government Guides Local Science and Technology Development Fund Projects ( 2022ZY0066 ), the Innovative Entrepreneurship Training Program of Inner Mongolia ( 202310129016 , 202210129017 , 202210129028 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of NCDs. (a) TEM picture. (b) HR-TEM picture, (c) particle diameter, (d) XRD spectra, (e) Raman spectra, and (f) FTIR spectra.

Physical–chemical, photoluminescence, and optical sensing characteristics of NCDs. UV–vis absorption and emission spectra and emission images of NCDs are shown in (a); (b) pictures of NCDs in the visible vs. 365 nm UV; (c) shows the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and (d) PL 3D image of NCDs; (e) and (f) show the stability of NCDs under different NaCl, pH; (g) shows the fluorescence quench of NCDs under different metal ions; (h) Fe3+ from 37.5 to 300 μM addition to NCDs results in a fluorescence response; (i) a relation curve links changes in fluorescence intensity to varying Fe3+ concentrations.
(a) schematic preparation of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels, (b, c) SEM images, (d) FT-IR spectra, (e-g) C1s and O1s in the XPS survey spectrum during a broad scan.

(a) Schematic of the abrupt extinction of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels, (b, c) SEM images, (d) FT-IR spectra, (e) Fe3+ concentration and NCDs-SP-MCC-gels fluorescence intensity (the image on the right side is the fluorescent quench of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels at various levels of Fe3+).

Effect of (a) the initial Fe3+ (absorptive capability:0.05 g; pH: 3.0; Temperature: 25 °C; Time: 120 min), (b) adsorption temperature (absorptive capability:0.05 g; pH: 3.0; Solution concentration: Fe3+: 1500 mg/g; Time: 120 min), (c) pH (absorptive capability: 0.05 g; Temperature: 45 °C; Solution concentration: Fe3+: 1500 mg/g; Time: 120 min), and (d) adsorption time (absorptive capability:0.05 g; Temperature: 45 °C; Solution concentration: Fe3+: 1500 mg/g; pH: 2.5). (e) Fitting Fe3+ adsorb data using the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models at various adsorption durations. (f) Fitting Fe3+ adsorption data using Freundlich and Langmuir adsorption isotherm models at various beginning concentrations.
(a) shows the digital photographs of the adsorption process of Fe3+ by NCDs-SP-MCC-gels, and Fig. 6 (b-g) shows the SEM images and EDS mapping of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels with adsorbed Fe3+.

XPS survey spectra (a) of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels after Fe3+adsorption. Fe2p (b) XPS high-resolution spectrogram after adsorption. O 1s (c), and C 1s (d) XPS high-resolution spectrogram of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels (front and rear adsorption).
Construction and formation of fluorescent NCDs-SP-MCC-gels.
Adsorption and detection mechanisms of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels for Fe(III).
Comparison of the adsorption and detection limited for fe3+ with previous biomass-based adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots crosslinked Salix psammophila microcrystalline cellulose interpenetrating hydrogel system for sensitive detection and efficient adsorption of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot 010018, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot 010018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Art Design, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot 010018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Art Design

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material Science and Art Design, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot 010018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Ecological Security and Green Development at Universities of Inner Mongolia Autonomous, Hohhot 010018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Ecological Security and Green Development at Universities of Inner Mongolia Autonomous

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Ecological Security and Green Development at Universities of Inner Mongolia Autonomous, Hohhot 010018, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Science, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot 010018, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To combat the contaminating effects of heavy-metal ions on water resources, we prepared blue photonitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) from Salix psammophila (SP) as a raw material, doped them into a microcrystalline cellulose hydrogel, and successfully created a composite hydrogel (NCDs-SP-MCC-gels) for concurrent fluorescence detection and Fe3+ adsorption. The NCDs-SP-MCC-gels was examined for their shape, chemical composition, fluorescence characteristics, and adsorption behaviors. Results demonstrated that the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels displayed a broad linear range of Fe3+ fluorescence response (200–1000 mg/L; detection limit = 3.90583 mg/L) and a high adsorption capacity for Fe3+ (610 mg/g). The adsorption data fitted the Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models well. Further research was conducted on Fe3+ detection and adsorption onto the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. This study proposes a green and sustainable way to create a material for the detection and absorption of heavy-metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advancement in global industrialization, heavy metals are being increasingly employed in industrial production [1]. Following their mining and smelting, numerous heavy-metal minerals are widely used in industrial processes, such as metal surface treatment, electronic component processing, equipment fabrication, printing, and dyeing. These procedures invariably result in the production of industrial effluents that contain a high amount of heavy-metal ions [2]. iron is a vital trace element for human health and is crucial for numerous biological functions, including DNA and RNA synthesis, enzyme catalysis, cellular metabolism, and electron transport activities [3–5]. Therefore, Fe3+ is essential for the majority of species, and its abundance or deficit causes various clinical diseases involving iron transport, storage, and balance [6,7]. Owing to its various functions, either advantageous or detrimental, Fe3+ detection is crucial. Activated carbon [8,9], ion exchange resins [10,11], biochar [12,13], polymer brushes [14], bioadsorbents [15,16], and hydrogels [17,18] have been created for the remediation of water contamination caused by heavy-metal ions.

The crosslinked three-dimensional (3D) network structure of hydrogels allows them to absorb a considerable amount of water while retaining their original structure [19]. Hydrogels must possess the following attributes: a chain structure that can be crosslinked or entangled via hydrogen bonds, electrostatic forces, or chemical interactions and a surface that is covered with a substantial amount of hydrophilic functional groups and accessible to water [20]. Fe3+ in wastewater can electrostatically attach to, be hydrogen bonded with, or chelate with a hydrogel owing to the hydrophilic active groups present on the hydrogel matrix surface for collecting heavy-metal ions in the water [21]. The hydrogel may also be readily isolated from wastewater because it is insoluble and maintains its stability in water, preventing subsequent contamination. Owing to these characteristics, hydrogels have attracted considerable attention as a brand-new class of metal ion adsorbents. Chirag [22] developed a cost-effective and environmentally friendly hydrogel comprising silk fibroin(SF) and polyethyleneimine(PEI) for the treatment of metal ions in water solutions. The SF/PEI hydrogel displayed high adsorption capabilities of 163.9, 185.2, 169.5, 125.0, 140.8, and 200.0 mg/g for Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, and Ag+, respectively. However, these hydrogel adsorbents have major drawbacks. The synthetic polymers utilized as hydrogel matrices are nonbiodegradable and pose a hazard to living things. Furthermore, the beneficial nanoparticles used in hydrogel adsorbents are often expensive and difficult to apply in large quantities. Natural biomass provides a plentiful, sustainable, and ecologically benign raw resource to make hydrogels for heavy-metal ion adsorption [23].

Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that can be synthesized by simply hydrothermally carbonizing polysaccharides derived from biomass. They have the benefits of being environmentally friendly and having low biotoxicity and many active sites [24,25]. Many heavy-metal ions can specifically quenchthe fluorescence emission of CDs owing to complex formation and electron transport between the functional groups on the surface and the core of CDs, which resembles graphite [26,27]. These characteristics of CDs imply their ability to capture heavy-metal ions in water. Also they can also be used to detect heavy metal ions in the water by lowering their fluorescence intensity.. Previous studies have suggested using CDs on various substrates, such as membranes, fibers, and gels [28,29]. Zou et al. [30] found that CDs could be used to detect Hg2+ in water by loading silane coupling agent on TEMPO-oxidized cellulose. Using a biosynthetic technique, Lv et al. [31] incorporated CDs onto cellulose from bacteria membranes and obtained a fluorescent response that was sensitive to Fe3+.

Herein, we prepared blue photo carbon quantum dots from SP as a raw material, doped them into the interior of SP-MCC-gels, and successfully synthesized a composite hydrogel with integrated detection and adsorption properties. Scheme 1 depicts the preparation of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. The morphology, structure, synthesis, and heavy-metal ions adsorption properties of this hydrogel were characterized and tested. In addition, the fluorescence response of carbon quantum dots to Fe3+ ions and the model of the adsorption mechanism of the hydrogel for heavy-metal Fe3+ were thoroughly analyzed. This study simplifies the creation of hydrogels for the treatment and visible surveillance of heavy-metal ions in the environment. The proposed synthesis and application of this hydrogel are nontoxic, environmentally friendly, and sustainable. This study provides an innovative direction for the development and transformation of biomass waste resources.

SP was procured from China’s Kabuki Desert and used in this study. In brief, drying and crushing, take 100–120 powder for reserve. NaClO2, NaOH, urea, HCl, FeCl3·7H2O, HAC (99.5 %), ethylenediamine (EDA), epichlorohydrin (ECH), and NaCl were obtained from Tianjin Kemao Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. KOH, HNO3, C2H5OH, MnCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, HgSO4, ZnSO4, CuSO4, Cr(NO3)3, MgSO4, CaCl2, Ni(CH3COO)2, BaCl2, and (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2 were acquired from Macklin, China. Deionized (DI) water was prepared in the laboratory.

SP (2.0 g) and EDA (0.5 mL) were mixed with 60 mL of DI water and stirred to create a homogenous solution, which was hydrothermally treated for 16 h at 180 °C in an autoclave. The solution was then cooled and purified in water for 24 h using a dialysis membrane upon the completion of the reaction. Finally, a freezing and drying oven was used to obtain NCDs.

SP-MCC-gels was prepared using a previously reported method with modifications [32]. In brief, 1.0 g of SP-MCC was gradually added to DI water under vigorous stirring to produce a homogenous solution. Afterward, 3.0 g of urea and 1.75 g of NaOH were added, followed by 2 mL of the crosslinker epichlorohydrin. After being heated to 55 °C for 2 h to complete the polymerization, the reaction was agitated for 15 min. The resulting aerogel was then rinsed with DI water for 48 h to remove the unreacted chemicals after cooling. The manufactured SP-MCC-gels was then dipped into a 0.5 g/L NCDs solution. The fluorescent aerogel was freeze-dried after 48 h and rinsed with DI water three times.

The probe solution, i.e., an NCDs solution (2 mL, 0.5 g/L), was placed in a quartz cuvette. Subsequently, the NCDs solution was supplemented with various quantities of Fe3+ and the fluorescence spectra were captured. A slice of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels was placed in the quartz cuvette with 2 mL of DI water, and metal ion salts were introduced progressively to capture the fluorescence spectra of the gel in the presence of the test cations.

In brief, 0.05 g of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels and 50 mL of an Fe3+ solution were mixed in a 100 mL conical flask, and the mixture was agitated at 25 °C in a water-bath oscillator. The supernatant was filtered using a filter membrane (0.45 µm) following adsorption equilibration, and the filtrate was assessed via flame atomic absorption. The effects of pH (1.5–3.5), contact duration (0–120 min), starting Fe3+ concentration (200–1700 mg/L), and temperature (298, 303, 308, 313, 318, and 323 K) were assessed to determine the adsorption capabilities of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels aerogel. The Fe3+ adsorption capacity (Qe ) of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
Q e
=
(
C 0
-
C e
) V
M
,
where Qe represents the adsorption capacity of the sample, mg/g; Ce represents the equilibrium concentration of the heavy-metal ion, mg/L; C0 represents the initial concentration of the heavy-metal ion solution, mg/L; V represents the volume of the heavy-metal ion solution, L; and M is the amount of the adsorbent, g.

Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models are frequently used to match the experimental adsorption data. These models, represented by Eqs (2) and (3), were used to investigate the probable rate-controlling phases of adsorption.
(2)
log
Q e
-
Q t
= l o g
Q e
-
K 1
t
2.303
,
(3)
t
Q t
=
1
K 2
Q
e
2
+
t
Q e
,
where Qe and qt represent the amounts of heavy-metal ions adsorbed (mg/g) at equilibrium and at time t (min), respectively; K1 (min−1) represents the pseudo-first-order rate constant; and K2 g·(mg/min)−1 represents the rate constant of the pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetic equation. log(Qe-Qt), t/Qt represents the Y axis, and t represents the X axis, The data were linearly fitted, and K1 and K2 were obtained from the slope and intercept, respectively.

Eqs (4) and (5) are the formulas corresponding to the Langmuir and Freundlich models, respectively:
(4)
Q e
=
Q m
K L
C e
1 +
K L
C e
,
(5)
ln
Q e
=
1 n
ln
C e
+ ln
K F
,
where Qe and Qm (mg/g) represent the amount of adsorbed Fe3+ by the SP-MCC-gels at equilibrium and the maximum adsorption capacity; Ce (mg/L) represents the equilibrium concentration of Fe3+; KL and KF represent the Langmuir and Freundlich constants, respectively; and n represents the Freundlich empirical parameter.

Adsorption studies were conducted at 298, 303, 308, 313, 318, and 323 K to examine the effect of temperature on the Fe3+ adsorption by the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. The thermodynamic values of ΔG, ΔH, and ΔS for Fe3+ adsorption on the adsorbents were determined based on the van’t Hoff equation (Eqs (6)–(9)):
(6)
K c
=
1000 C
a
C e
,
(7)
Δ G = - R T ln
K c
,
(8)
ln
K c
=
Δ S
R
-
Δ H
RT
,
(9)
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S ,
where Kc (mL/g) represents the partition coefficient; Ca (mg/L) and Ce (mg/L) represent the adsorbed amount of Fe3+ for the SP-MCC-gels and the equilibrium concentration of Fe3+, respectively; T represents the temperature; R represents the gas constant (8.314 J/mol/ K); and ΔG (kJ/mol), ΔH (kJ/ mol), and ΔS (J/K/mol) represent the Gibbs free energy changes, enthalpy change, and entropy change, respectively.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Phenom Pro; Funa Scientific Instruments (Shanghai) Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was operated at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. A small number of samples were placed on a conductive adhesive and then gold-sprayed (small-ion sputtering instrument, Beijing Zhongke Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) for morphological observation. An FEI Tecnai S-200 kV transmission electron microscope was used for twin-field emission high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). The sample was diluted with anhydrous ethanol, dried in a copper mesh, and tested. X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests were conducted using an X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean Alpha-1, Shanghai SIBG Instrument System Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) under the following conditions: Cu Ka radiation at a generator voltage of 40 kV and current of 40 mA. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using a Perkin Elmer 65 infrared spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Instruments Co., Ltd., Waltham, Massachusetts, USA); the cumulative number of scans was 32, and the resolution was 1 cm−1. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a UV spectrophotometer (TU1950-1950, Beijing General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using an F98 spectro fluorophotometer (Shanghai Prism Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) equipped with a 150-W Xe lamp. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer with an Al (single) X-ray source. The charging effect was corrected by adjusting the binding energy of C1s to 284.6 eV. Fe3+ were determined using an PE-900F flame atomic absorption spectrometer.

TEM images showed that the NCDs were evenly distributed and circular (Fig. 1 a) and had a crystal structure with a lattice distance of 0.19 nm and a mean dimension of 3.0 nm (Fig. 1b and c). Owing to the extremely disordered graphitic structure, the XRD pattern of the prepared NCDs (Fig. 1d) displayed a large peak centered at 2θ = 22.4° [33]. The Raman spectrum of NCDs is shown in Fig. 1e, in which the vibrations of the sp3-hybridized carbon atoms were related to the D band (also known as the disordered band) at 1353 cm−1. The prominent peak inthe G band appeared at approximately 1568 cm−1, which is common for effectively structured graphite individual crystals; this indicated that the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms were moving in plane [34,35]. The NCDs were structurally similar to graphite, the ID/IG of which is 0.98 [36]. FTIR spectroscopy was used to study the surface function that occurs on NCDs. The prominent peaks at approximately 3300–3500 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 1f) can be related to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. The asymmetric and symmetric CH stretching vibrations caused the peaks observed at 2927 and 2856 cm−1, respectively. The peak observed at 1650 cm−1 demonstrated the presence of CO in NCDs. The band observed at 1290 cm−1 indicated the existence of CN bonds, and the peak observed at approximately 1050 cm−1 confirmed the presence of COC bonds [37]. These findings demonstrated that functional groups such as –NH, CH, CN, and CO exist in the NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing capabilities of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs solutions showed peaks at 265 and 330 nm (Fig. 2 a). The CC π-π* transitions and n-π* transitions between oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups and sp2 aromatic domains caused these two absorbance peaks [38,39]. An intense peak appeared at a maximum emission wavelength of 465 nm in response to excitation at 398 nm. Fig. 2(b) shows that when a 200-mg/g NCDs solution was irradiated by a 365-nm UV lamp, its color changed from a light brown to bright blue. Fig. 2(c) shows the emission spectra of NCDs at excitation wavelengths from 370 to 450 nm. The maximum level of fluorescence was observed at 400 nm. For carbon-based fluorescent materials, fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength are usually dependent on excitation [40–42]. This phenomenon can be primarily attributed to the emissive states created by various functional groups or particle size dispersion [43]. The relation between the excitation and emission wavelengths of NCDs was also immediately discernible through their 3D fluorescence patterns, as shown in Fig. 2(d). PL exhibits a strong correlation with the surface flaws of NCDs, which might overcome the size-dependent inter-band transition [44]. Fluorescence-based applications aim for excellent PL stability. Accordingly, the fluorescence stability of the NCDs solution was tested at various pH levels and NaCl concentrations. The NCDs solution exhibited exceptional PL stability at high levels of sodium chloride up to 1 mol/L (Fig. 2e). Then, we tested the fluorescence stability of the obtained CDs in different pH values from 1 to 11. As shown in Fig. 2(f), the change of the fluorescence intensity is small from pH of 3 to 11, only strong acid (pH = 1) lead to strong quench of fluorescence, this may result from the protonation reaction of the NCDs under acidic conditions. This result indicates that the CDs can keep good fluorescence intensity in a wide pH value. From Fig. 2(f) we can see that in the acidic conditions, fluorescence spectrum generated red shift. For the analysis of NCDs selectivity, the PL spectra of the NCDs obtained under the influence of different metal cations were examined. 400 μM Various metal cations including Fe3+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Mg2+ and Pb2+ (Fig. 2g) reacted with the NCDs. The PL spectra of the NCDs containing various Fe3+ ions under UV illumination at 398 nm are shown in (Fig. 2h). The peak of PL intensity was observed at 453 nm. It can be clearly seen from Figure that the fluorescence intensity decreases with increase of Fe3+ content. According to the slope of the line that shows the linear relation between the relative peak PL intensity (F0/F) and Fe3+, a correlation value of 0.99029 was obtained, indicating that the linearity was excellent (Fig. 2i).

Fig. S1(a) shows a schematic diagram of the carbon dots before and after quenching, from which it can be concluded that the carbon dots emit blue light under UV–vis lamp irradiation before quenching, and the solution does not fluoresce under UV–vis lamp irradiation after addition of Fe3+ ions and quenching occurs. By FTIR we can find that the amine peak strength of the NCDs solution in Fig. S1(b), becomes weaker at the 3300–3500 cm−1 peak position compared to the NCDs after the reaction with Fe3+. This side can prove that Fe3+ reacts mainly with the amino groups in NCDs. The functional groups and elemental compositions of the surface of NCDs were determined by XPS. In the full-spectrum scanning XPS of NCDs Fig. S2(a), there are three major peaks at 284.8, 399.9, and 532.4 eV, belonging to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s Fig. S2(b) has four carbon bond compositions, which correspond to 284.8 eV for CC, 285.7 eV for CN, 286.8 eV for CO and 288.1 eV of CO [45]. Fig. S2(c) shows the fine spectra of N peaks of NCDs, including three peaks of amino N (398.5 eV), amide N (399.3 eV) and graphite N (400.3 eV)[46]. The quench mechanism of NCDs is mainly a fluorescence resonance energy transfer phenomenon, since Fe3+ will chelate with the large amount of amino groups enriched on the surface of NCDs.

Fig. 3 (a) depicts the preparation of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. SEM images of the freeze-dried NCDs-SP-MCC-gels with a 3D network porosity structure are displayed in Fig. 3(b and c), showing the network structure of NCDs with embedded SP-MCC-gels. Further magnification revealed that the skeleton of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels was mostly comprised linked, wall-shaped SP-MCC-gels. Fig. 3(d) shows the FTIR spectra of SP-MCC-gels, NCDs-SP-MCC-gels, and NCDs. NaClO2 oxidation of the hydroxyl groups on the cellulose surfaces was the reason for the distinctive CO absorbance band of carboxyl and aldehyde appearing at 1650 cm−1 for the SP-MCC-gels. A prominent absorption band in the spectra of NCDs observed at 1290 cm−1 was ascribed to CN stretching vibration. The sharp bands observed at 1050 cm−1 were possibly caused by COC stretching vibration. All the aforementioned bands of NCDs were also found in the spectrum of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels [47]. Spectral comparison between NCDs-SP-MCC-gelsand SP-MCC-gelsrevealed two new absorption bands at 1650 and 1603 cm−1, corresponding to amide I and II, respectively [48]. These bands partially covered the CO stretching band of the carboxyl group of NaClO2-oxidized SP-MCC-gels at 1650 cm−1 [49]. This finding suggests that the carboxyl groups of SP-MCC-gels were condensed by the amino-rich, hydrothermally produced NCDs to form amide bands these NCDs were subsequently embedded to act for the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels [50]. All the spectra showed –NH2 or –OH stretching vibrations at 3000–3400 cm−1, with the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels exhibiting a band strength higher than those of the SP-MCC-gels and NCDs. This difference in intensity can be attributed to the numerous amino groups on the surface of NCDs that allow NCDs to create covalent connections with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the SP-MCC-gels via a nucleophilic reaction in addition to self-assembling with SP-MCC-gels through electrostatic and hydrogen interactions.

The NCDs-SP-MCC-gels were analyzed via XPS to determine their chemical contents. Fig. 3(e) illustrates distinct peaks at 285, 400, and 532 eV, which were attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectra is shown in Fig. 3(f and g) for the analysis of the bond configuration of C and O atoms in the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. The peaks observed at 284.6, 285.6, 286.4, and 287.7 eV were identified as CC, CN, COH, and CO, respectively. Compared with SP-MCC Fig. S2(d), the CN peak at 285.6 eV appeared in the spectra of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels, and the CO peak of SP-MCC was shifted from 287.4 eV to 287.7 eV, which was due to the shift of the peak position due to the presence of the CN peak. Meanwhile, the positions of the carbon peaks of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels are found in NCDs, as shown in Fig. S2(b). Fig. S2(c) shows the fine spectra of N peaks of NCDs, including three peaks of amino N (398.5 eV), amide N (399.3 eV) and graphite N (400.3 eV). This indicated that the NCDs were prepared by hydrothermal reaction and had N-doped carbocation cores and abundant nitrogenous oxygenated groups on the surface, and this also revealed the existence of amide bond cross-linking between the amino group on the surface of NCDs and the carboxyl group on the surface of SP-MCC. The two peaks observed at 531.7 and 532.2 eV corresponded to the CO and CO bonds, respectively, and could be fitted into the O1s spectra of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. The XPS spectra also confirmed that the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels was successfully prepared.

Fig. 4 (a) depicts a diagram of the quench of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels (diameter: 2 cm) immersed in 100 mL of a 1 g/L Fe3+ solution. According to the graph, the fluorescence of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels disappeared after immersion in Fe3+, indicating that Fe3+ exerted a good quench effect on the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. Fig. 4(b and c) shows the SEM images of Fe3+ adsorption by the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels; Fe3+ appeared to be uniformly dispersed in the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels.

The FTIR spectra of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels and SP-MCC-gels-Fe3+ are shown in Fig. 4(d). The NH and OH stretching vibration bands of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels slightly diminished after Fe3+ adsorption. This finding demonstrated that the heavy-metal ions were adsorbed onto the amino and hydroxyl groups as the adsorption sites. After Fe3+ addition, the amide I and II bands (1650 and 1603 cm−1) disappeared and a new sharp band (1642 cm−1) developed. This result showed that Fe3+ reacted with the amide bond.

The fluorescence responses of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels to Fe3+ are shown in Fig. 4(e). Fig. 4(e) demonstrates a broad concentration limit of 200–1000 mg/L, indicating a strong linear relation (R2 = 0.99796) between the concentration of Fe3+ and the relative PL peak intensity ((F0/F) of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. For the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels, the detection limit for Fe3+ was 3.90583 mg/L. The fluorescence intensity gradient of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels is visible in the Fig. 4(e) on the right side, indicating the progressive quench fluorescence of these gels under various Fe3+ concentrations. The NCDs-SP-MCC-gels exhibited a relatively stable and undisturbed fluorescence emission intensity in the working pH range (approximately 1.6–7.2) of Fe3+ adsorption, as shown in Fig. S3, allowing for the simultaneous detection of heavy-metal ions by the composite hydrogel under acidic conditions.

Four aspects of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels, i.e., initial concentration, pH, temperature, and adsorption time, were investigated to determine its optimum Fe3+ adsorption performance. Fig. 5 (ad) shows that the ideal adsorption conditions were attained when the initial concentration of Fe3+ was 1500 mg/g, pH was 2.5, temperature was 45 °C, and adsorption time was 60 min. Furthermore, the maximum adsorption amount was 610 mg/g. The adsorption capacities of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels for Fe3+ were compared with other biomass-based adsorbents obtained from previous papers, as summarized in Table 1 . The results suggest that NCDs-SP-MCC-gels has superior adsorption capacity and can be an effective adsorbent for removing Fe3+ from waste water.

A kinetic study was conducted for thoroughly analyzing the system. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels achieved equilibrium after approximately 1 h. When the rate data were fitted to pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order model, as shown in Fig. 5(e), Fig. S4 and Fig. S5, Pseudo-secondary model more consistent with adsorption modeling, a linear correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9930) was obtained, the fit was worse when using a pseudo-first-order model. Therefore, the adsorption process adheres to the pseudo-second-order model. The fitting results showed that the rate-limiting step was not the resistance of the boundary layer [59]. Instead, chemical adsorption predominated during Fe3+ capture [60]. The interaction between the adsorbents and adsorbate was then elucidated using an isotherm model. As the concentration increased, the adsorption capacity increased until it reached saturation. Fig. 5(f) Fig. S6 and Fig. S7 provides the data that were fitted to the Langmuir and Freundlich models and the specific fitting parameters are listed in Table S2. At 318 K, the R2 value of the Freundlich model outperformed that of the Langmuir model. The fitting results were in agreement with multilayer heterogeneous adsorption. In addition, all the 1/n values were in the range of 0 to 1, as predicted for a system that can efficiently adsorption aqueous Fe3+ ions.

The effect of temperature on the stability of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels was investigated to further assess its adsorption capabilities. Table 2 illustrates how the adsorption capacity increased with increasing temperature from 293 to 323 K. The thermodynamic parameters were established to comprehend the adsorption procedure. According to the observed positive ΔH value, the removal of Fe3+ is endothermic and the adsorption of Fe3+ adsorption is dominated by entropy [61]. All the ΔG values were negative. The number of negative ΔG values increased with increasing temperature, indicating the enhancement of adsorption at high temperatures [62]. Therefore, the removal of Fe3+ during adsorption is probably aided by the porous structure and different functional groups.

Fig. 6 (a) illustrates the absorption process of Fe3+ on NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. The hydrogel, with a diameter of approximately 2 cm and a height of around 0.5 cm, was immersed in a 50 mL solution containing Fe3+ at a concentration of 1000 mg/L. After 12 h, the solution became light yellow, indicating that the heavy metal ions were absorbed. To further demonstrate the adsorption of metal ions by NCDs-SP-MCC-gels, we performed SEM-EDS elemental characterization analysis (Fig. 6b–g). The images show that the elements C, N, and O are uniformly dispersed in the structure of NCDs-SP-MMC-gels. In addition, it can also be seen that Fe ions are uniformly dispersed in the samples after adsorption by the adsorbent, which indicates that Fe metal ions are successfully adsorbed on the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels.

The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 7 a) showed that after adsorption, peaks of several chemical elements were observed for the surface of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels. The high-resolution areas of Fe 2p, O 1s, and C 1s are shown in Fig. 7(b)–(d) before and after NCDs-SP-MCC-gels adsorption. Fe was present on the surface of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels in two valence states (Fe3+ and Fe2+), suggesting that Fe3+ ions were partly reduced as shown in Fig. 7(b). Fe3+ peaks were found between 710.2 and 723.7 eV, and Fe2+ bands were mostly observed between 708 and 722 eV. The relative quantity of Fe3+ was greater than that of Fe2+, indicating that the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels is likely to accumulate iron oxides. The O 1s and C 1s spectra shown in Fig. 7(c) and 7(d) also supported this theory. The Fe3+ adsorbed by the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels showed a new peak corresponding to iron oxides in the O1s spectra at 531 eV. Considerably less CN spectral intensity was observed. This finding implied that many O electrons underwent complexation with Fe3+ and that the active sites for the adsorption of Fe3+ included the amino, amide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups [63,64].

We prepared blue photo carbon quantum dots from SP as raw material, doped them into the interior SP microcrystalline cellulose hydrogel, and successfully synthesized a composite hydrogel with integrated detection and adsorption properties. The shape, chemical composition, synthesis, and optical characteristics of the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels were examined. Its network demonstrated a crosslinked 3D structure where NCDs crosslinked with SP-MCC-gels via amide bonds. Under UV light excitation, the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels emitted stable blue fluorescence. The fluorescent response and adsorption of NCDs-SP-MCC-gels for Fe3+ were systematically investigated. Owing to the nonradiative energy transformation between NCDs and Fe3+, the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels displayed a broad range of linear fluorescent responses for Fe3+ (200–1000 mg/L). Owing to the chemical and physical reactions between the N and O groups of the hydrogel and Fe3+, the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels demonstrated a high adsorption capacity of 610 mg/g for Fe3+. All these findings show that the NCDs-SP-MCC-gels has great prospects for the adsorption and monitoring of heavy-metal ions.

Boyun Wang: Writing – original draft. Yuan Zhong: Validation. Zhechen Liu: Formal analysis. Wanqi Zhang: Funding acquisition. Zichu Hu: Supervision. Long Chen: Data curation. Ximing Wang: Writing – review & editing. Xiaotao Zhang: Supervision, Project administration, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Interdisciplinary Research Fund of Inner Mongolia Agricultural University (BR22-15-02), the Central Government Guides Local Science and Technology Development Fund Projects (2022ZY0066), the Innovative Entrepreneurship Training Program of Inner Mongolia (202310129016, 202210129017, 202210129028)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110410.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
